planting.note: Plant spring or fall; shelter the showy spring foliage from strong sun and wind.

directions: Set the upright tree at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch the root zone. Spring leaves unfurl with crinkled, cream-and-pink margins around a green center, an unusual crepe-paper texture that settles to green by summer. Give it filtered light to protect the delicate early color.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall into well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in rich, well-drained soil and water steadily while establishing. The finely lobed burgundy leaves hold their color through summer exceptionally well and are more heat-tolerant than many red uprights. Autumn deepens the foliage to bright crimson.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a vigorous upright that takes more sun than most red cultivars.

directions: Set the tree at grade in deep, well-drained soil and allow room for a vigorous upright crown. Its distinctive deeply lobed leaves roll their edges downward and hold a rich purple-red through summer, turning crimson in fall. This robust cultivar makes an excellent specimen tree in larger gardens.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; this full moon maple is a touch hardier and prefers cooler summers.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil and shelter from hot, drying wind. The rounded leaves are so deeply cut they resemble fern fronds, staying green all summer before igniting in brilliant scarlet, orange, and gold. Small red spring flowers dangle beneath the emerging foliage.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; prefers cool, moist conditions and shelter from wind.

directions: Set the tree at grade in rich, moisture-retentive soil that still drains freely, and mulch generously. The large, broad, vine-shaped green leaves make a lush summer canopy and turn spectacular shades of crimson, orange, and gold in fall. It matures into a broad, rounded specimen for a woodland-edge setting.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; often staked to build height before the branches weep.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in cool, moist, well-drained soil and stake the leader early if you want an arching cascade rather than a ground-hugging mat. The deeply dissected green leaves flow downward like a waterfall and turn brilliant orange and red in autumn. It is striking trained over a boulder or wall.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a compact cultivar well suited to containers and small beds.

directions: Plant the dwarf tree at grade in well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist while young. A dense, compact sport of 'Bloodgood', it packs tightly clustered bright red-maroon leaves onto a bushy, upright frame. Its small size makes it ideal for patios, containers, and tight garden corners.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; grown for a spectacular flush of spring color.

directions: Set the tree at nursery depth in fertile, well-drained soil and give it filtered light. Spring foliage erupts in a vivid fire-red and pink that few maples can match before settling to green-bronze for summer, with a second orange-red show in fall. Keep it well watered so the brilliant new growth does not scorch.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a classic bonsai subject prized for spring color.

directions: Plant at grade in moist, well-drained soil with morning sun and afternoon shade. New spring leaves emerge an intense scarlet-red, mature to reddish-green through summer, and return to bright red in fall. Its refined branching and color make it a longtime favorite for containers and bonsai training.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a small cultivar with vivid spring foliage.

directions: Plant the compact tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist. Its name means 'red dancing girl', and the small spring leaves open a brilliant fiery red before greening in summer. Give it some direct sun to intensify color while protecting the fine foliage from harsh midday heat.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; noted for brilliant spring and autumn color.

directions: Set the tree at grade in fertile, moist, well-drained soil with filtered light. Spring leaves flush an intense crimson-pink, mature to green through summer, and light up scarlet again in fall. Its dense, twiggy habit makes it a compact, colorful choice for smaller gardens and containers.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall into moist, well-drained soil with wind protection.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and let the tiered, gently weeping branches form a graceful cascading crown. The deeply divided green leaves flow along arching limbs and turn gold, orange, and crimson in fall. Its layered, pendulous habit gives an elegant, airy silhouette in the landscape.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; usually staked to set the height of its curtain of foliage.

directions: Plant at grade and stake the leader to the desired height, since the branches hang straight down without an arch. The green summer leaves drape in a narrow, flowing curtain that turns bright orange and red in autumn. It works beautifully cascading beside steps, a wall, or a water feature.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a linear-leaf cultivar for small spaces and containers.

directions: Plant the compact tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil and keep the root zone cool. Its unusual strap-like, thread-thin red leaves give a soft, feathery texture that mellows toward green-red in summer heat. Autumn brings warm gold and orange tones to the delicate foliage.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a graceful narrow-leaf form for compact gardens.

directions: Set the tree at grade in moist, well-drained soil with filtered light. Its name means 'harp strings', describing the very narrow, thread-like green leaves that give an airy, delicate texture. The foliage turns clear golden yellow in fall on an upright, refined frame suited to small gardens.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; valued for its glowing spring flush and reliable habit.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in fertile, well-drained soil and give it morning sun. Spring leaves emerge a soft pale orange-yellow edged in pink, mature to light green in summer, and finish gold and orange in fall. Its dense, upright oval form makes it an easy, colorful specimen for smaller yards.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; grown for bright winter bark like a refined coral-bark type.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil where winter light can catch the branches. Similar to 'Sango-kaku' but with finer twigs, its young bark glows salmon-red in the cold months, giving winter interest after the green summer leaves fall. Autumn foliage turns soft golden yellow before dropping to reveal the colored stems.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a true dwarf for containers, troughs, and bonsai.

directions: Plant the dwarf tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist in its small root run. Spring leaves emerge red-tinged, mature to green, and close the season in orange, gold, and red on a dense, twiggy frame. Its diminutive size makes it a superb choice for containers and small gardens.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a dense dwarf beloved for bonsai.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and give this very slow grower steady moisture. Its short internodes crowd overlapping green leaves in a shingled, layered pattern on a tight, bushy form. Fall turns the dense foliage gold and orange, and its compact structure makes it a classic bonsai and trough subject.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a narrow columnar dwarf for tight spaces.

directions: Plant the slender tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil and site it where its narrow upright form can be appreciated. A witch's-broom sport of 'Bloodgood', it holds bright burgundy-red leaves on a dense, columnar frame that fits narrow beds and containers. Fall deepens the color to crimson.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a narrow upright red for vertical accents.

directions: Set the tree at grade in well-drained soil and use its slim, upright habit as a vertical accent in tight spots. It holds deep red-purple foliage through summer better than many red uprights and finishes bright scarlet in fall. Give it some direct sun to keep the color rich.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; give afternoon shade to protect the veined foliage.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in moist, well-drained soil with dappled light. One of the veined 'Ghost' series, it carries deep purple-black leaves boldly marked with darker veining that stands out in the shade garden. It holds color well through summer and turns bright crimson in fall.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; one of the more heat- and sun-tolerant red uprights.

directions: Plant at grade in rich, well-drained soil and water regularly while establishing. Its dark purple-red leaves resist the summer fading that afflicts many red cultivars, holding deep color even in warm regions. Fall brings a brilliant crimson finish on a strong upright frame.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a linear-leaf form with soft, airy texture.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in moist, well-drained soil with filtered light. Its narrow, ribbon-like leaves emerge pinkish-red, settle to green in summer, and turn warm gold and orange in fall. The fine, strap-leaf texture and spreading habit give a soft, cascading appearance.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a weeping laceleaf noted for orange-toned new growth.

directions: Plant the cascading tree at grade in well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. Successive flushes of new growth emerge orange-red over a base of deep red-green, giving a layered, two-tone effect on the weeping mound. Fall turns the finely cut foliage a vivid orange-scarlet.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a compact weeping laceleaf with vivid spring color.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in well-drained soil and let the branches build a dense, mounding cascade. Spring foliage flushes bright orange-red, matures to green-red in summer, and returns to fiery orange in fall. Its full, layered mound of finely dissected leaves makes a striking container or focal specimen.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in dappled shade to protect the pale variegation.

directions: Plant at grade in cool, moist, well-drained soil and shield from hot afternoon sun. Spring leaves open in soft blends of cream, pink, and pale green that give the tree a gentle, luminous look. Provide filtered light and steady moisture to keep the delicate variegated foliage from scorching.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a small cultivar with pink-toned spring growth.

directions: Plant the compact tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil with filtered light. Spring leaves emerge a soft shrimp-pink and orange before greening for summer, then turn warm orange and gold in fall. Its modest size suits containers and small garden beds.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in a sheltered spot with afternoon shade for the variegation.

directions: Plant at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and protect from drying wind and harsh sun. The green leaves are irregularly margined in cream and pink, similar to 'Butterfly' but with a warmer, rosier edge. Prune out any green-reverting shoots to keep the variegated display strong.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a sturdy green cultivar tolerant of more sun.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in well-drained soil and water steadily while establishing. Its thick, rich green leaves resist summer scorch better than many cultivars, making it a dependable choice for sunnier sites. Autumn brings a bright, pure pumpkin-orange color to the dense, rounded crown.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; an upright with dark, long-holding purple foliage.

directions: Set the tree at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and give it good light for best color. Its deeply cut leaves hold a dark blackish-purple through summer, staying darker than many red uprights. Fall turns the foliage bright crimson on a vigorous, upright frame.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; grown for green winter bark as well as foliage.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in well-drained soil where winter light can reveal the stems. The green counterpart to the coral-bark maples, its young twigs glow bright jade-green in winter after the leaves drop. Bright green summer foliage turns golden yellow in fall on an upright, rounded tree.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; a weeping laceleaf with a distinctive twisting habit.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and let the contorted, gyrating branches build an irregular cascading mound. The finely dissected green leaves ride branches that twist and spiral, giving unusual winter structure once bare. Fall brings warm orange and red to the lacy foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in any average garden soil.

directions: Set the shrub at nursery depth in fertile, well-drained ground and water it in well. Prune out old canes after flowering to keep the crown open and vigorous. Round pom-pom clusters open lime-green and turn pure white in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding grade and keep the soil consistently moist during establishment. Prune sparingly after bloom, removing only crossing or damaged wood. Rows of white snowball clusters ride the horizontal branches in spring for a striking tiered display.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soils.

directions: This adaptable native can be grown as a tall shrub or limbed up into a small tree, so give it depth to develop. Water through the first summer and it will handle drought thereafter. Flat white flower clusters in spring give way to blue-black fruit relished by birds.

planting.note: Plant in full sun in spring or fall for repeat bloom.

directions: Plant this compact reblooming lilac in full sun and deadhead after the spring show to prompt later flushes. It stays rounded and needs little structural pruning. Fragrant lavender-pink panicles open in spring and return intermittently through summer.

planting.note: Plant in full sun in spring or fall.

directions: Plant this graceful, fine-textured lilac in full sun where its arching habit can show. It tolerates warmer climates better than common lilac and needs only light pruning. Slender, fragrant pale-lilac panicles open along the airy branches in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in full sun in spring or fall; ideal for small gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf, cold-hardy lilac in full sun where its compact rounded form fits tight spaces. It needs minimal pruning and resists mildew well. Deep pink buds open to fragrant pink flowers on a plant that stays neat without effort.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for heaviest bloom.

directions: Plant this vigorous shrub at nursery depth in full sun and average soil. Prune right after flowering by removing the oldest canes at ground level, since blooms form on year-old wood. Arching branches erupt in bright yellow flowers before the leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set this upright, heavy-flowering selection in full sun and water through establishment. Renew it by cutting a portion of the oldest stems to the ground after bloom. Deep golden-yellow flowers wreath the branches densely in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant this more compact, upright forsythia in full sun where its dense flowering shows off in smaller borders. Prune only after bloom to keep the tidy form. Flowers crowd the stems from base to tip in brilliant yellow in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this low, mounding shrub at nursery depth as a front-of-border edger or informal groundcover. Prune lightly after flowering to keep it dense. Clouds of small white flowers cover the arching stems in late spring, and the foliage turns burgundy in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this spreading dwarf as a groundcover or edging in sun to light shade. It needs little pruning and stays neatly mounded. Pink-and-white flowers smother the low stems in spring, and the foliage flushes deep burgundy in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Use this low, wide-spreading deutzia to knit together the front of a bed or a shady slope. Shear lightly after flowering if you want to tidy the mound. Pure white flowers cover the arching stems in spring, followed by rich fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for best foliage color.

directions: Plant this fine-textured spirea in full sun where its golden, willowy foliage glows all season. Water through establishment and shear lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Tiny white flowers line the arching stems in early spring before the leaves emerge, and the foliage turns orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant this compact spirea in full sun to bring out the unusual blue-green summer foliage. Water regularly the first year and shear lightly to keep the tidy mound. White spring flowers cover the plant, and the leaves take on burgundy-purple tones in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant this rounded birchleaf spirea in full sun for the brightest golden foliage. It is low-maintenance and needs only a light shearing after bloom. Flat white flower clusters cover the plant in late spring above chartreuse leaves that turn orange and red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Give this arching bridal-wreath type room to fountain and plant it in full sun. It offers better disease resistance than the old form; thin the oldest canes after bloom to renew it. Cascading branches drip with clusters of white flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant this tough, cold-hardy species spirea in full sun and average soil. It is drought tolerant once established and needs only a light renewal pruning after bloom. Small rounded clusters of white flowers cover the low, mounded branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist soil; spreads by suckers.

directions: Plant this vigorous, suckering shrub where it can colonize, such as a slope or naturalized area, in moist soil. Cut it back hard in late winter to control size and refresh the ferny foliage. Large fluffy white plumes rise above the compound leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist ground; tolerates wet sites.

directions: Plant this upright, suckering meadowsweet in moist to wet soil where its spreading habit is welcome. Cut back in late winter to keep it dense and manage its spread. Narrow upright plumes of soft pink flowers top the willowy stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for darkest foliage.

directions: Plant in full sun at nursery depth so the deep burgundy foliage develops its richest color. Water through the first season and prune right after the main bloom to shape it. Rosy-pink funnel flowers cover the dark-leaved branches in late spring, with scattered rebloom in summer.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant so the crown sits level with the surrounding soil in a bed with sharp drainage. This dwarf selection carries full-size periwinkle-blue panicles on a compact frame and flowers all summer on new growth. Cut the whole plant back hard in early spring to encourage fresh flowering wood.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the rootball and twice as wide, then backfill and water in well. Deep violet-purple spikes draw butterflies from midsummer until frost. Remove spent flower heads to keep bloom coming and prune close to the ground each spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Give this vigorous shrub room and full sun in average, well-drained ground. Long dark-purple panicles open through summer and are a magnet for pollinators. Cut stems back to a low framework in early spring before new growth begins.

planting.note: Set into warmed spring soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with the base flush to the soil surface and mulch lightly. True blue flower spikes with orange eyes appear all summer on the current year's wood. Deadhead often and prune hard in early spring for the best show.

planting.note: Transplant after spring frosts end.

directions: Choose a sunny, freely draining site and water new plants until established. Rosy-pink arching panicles cover this compact English Butterfly series shrub through the warm months. Trim spent blooms and cut back close to the base each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the frost-free date.

directions: Site this large shrub in full sun with plenty of elbow room. Fragrant deep wine-purple spikes bloom heavily from midsummer to frost. Cut the plant back hard in early spring and deadhead through the season.

planting.note: Set out after all danger of frost in spring.

directions: Plant in a warm, sheltered spot with good drainage and room to spread. Rounded clusters of soft yellow, lilac-flushed flowers open in summer and carry a honey scent. Prune to a low framework in spring to renew the flowering shoots.

common_name: Flutterby Grande Blueberry Cobbler Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Transplant into warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Give this robust hybrid full sun and free-draining soil. Large blue-purple panicles bloom from early summer until frost and attract many butterflies. Cut back hard in early spring and shear spent heads to prolong the display.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant in full sun with the rootball flush to grade and water in well. Rich cranberry-magenta spikes bloom through summer and draw butterflies steadily. Remove faded flowers and prune hard each spring for vigorous rebloom.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost, in a warm sunny spot.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil where summers are warm and sunny. Clusters of clear blue flowers cover the gray-green foliage in late summer, prized by bees. Cut the stems back to a few inches in early spring to keep it dense and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun.

directions: Site in well-drained soil and full sun to keep the foliage color bright. Golden-chartreuse leaves set off sky-blue late-summer flowers that hum with bees. Shear back hard in early spring, since bloom forms on new wood.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant in full sun and light, fast-draining soil for the best silvery leaf color. Deep blue flowers gather along the stems in late summer and early fall. Prune close to the ground in spring to renew growth and flowering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set in full sun with excellent drainage and avoid soggy winter soil. Unusual soft-pink flowers open above green foliage in late summer, drawing pollinators. Cut the whole plant back to a low framework in early spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with the crown at grade. Yellow flowers brushed with orange-red centers appear all summer and show their brightest color in cooler weather. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the mound dense.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny, cool site.

directions: Plant in full sun but keep roots cool with mulch, since heat mutes the flower color. Deep red-orange flowers cover the low, spreading foliage through summer. Trim back by a third in early spring to encourage fresh bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Site in full sun in well-drained soil for the most flowers. Soft pale-pink single blooms appear over a low, spreading mound from summer into fall. Shear back lightly in early spring to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant in full sun to watch new growth open orange before deepening to wine-red. White flower clusters appear in early summer along the branches. Thin the oldest canes to the ground after bloom to keep color and vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for best foliage color.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, wearing gloves against the thorns. New leaves emerge rose-pink mottled on purple, and small yellow flowers hang beneath the stems in spring. Shear lightly after flowering to shape, or leave it to grow into a loose mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set this dwarf barberry in full sun to keep the crimson-red foliage rich and deep. Tiny yellow flowers appear in spring, followed by small red berries in fall. It needs little pruning, but a light shearing keeps the mound neat.

planting.note: Set out in early spring or fall while soil stays cool and moist.

directions: Plant with the root flare at grade in well-drained, slightly acidic soil and firm the backfill to remove air pockets. This dense rounded selection shears cleanly, so trim after the spring flush to keep a tidy outline. Tiny greenish-white flowers open in late spring and black drupes follow on female plants by summer.

planting.note: Best transplanted in spring or early fall into cool ground.

directions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root ball and settle the plant so the top of the roots sits just above the soil line. Its glossy dark leaves stay low and spreading, making it a good facer shrub or informal low hedge. Water deeply during dry spells and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Site this loose, mounding holly where its wider-than-tall habit has room to spread naturally. Avoid heavy clay and standing water, which quickly cause root problems, and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Only occasional pruning is needed since the plant fills in with soft, informal branching.

planting.note: Set into cool spring soil in a protected spot.

directions: This extremely slow, low cushion is ideal for rock gardens, troughs, and the front of a bed. Plant in gritty, sharply drained soil and keep it evenly moist until well rooted. Little pruning is required, and its tiny leaves give a bonsai-like fine texture year round.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very tolerant of heat and poor soils.

directions: This heavy-fruiting female selection grows into a small multi-stem tree loaded with translucent red berries. Plant in almost any well-drained soil and water through the first summer to establish deep roots. Once settled it is highly drought- and salt-tolerant; limb it up gradually to show off the pale bark.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; drought tolerant once established.

directions: Use this narrow male form as a vertical accent, a screen, or a soft substitute for a slender conifer. Because the tight column can splay in wind or snow, tie the stems loosely together or plant in a sheltered line. It tolerates heat, sandy soil, and salt spray with minimal care.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun for best color.

directions: This compact mound pushes out burgundy-tinted new growth that ages to green, giving color without shearing. Plant in well-drained soil and site in full sun to intensify the red tones. It makes an easy low hedge or foundation filler and needs little water once rooted.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in moist, acidic soil; tolerates wet sites.

directions: A fuller, less leggy inkberry that holds foliage down to the base better than the species. Plant in consistently moist, acidic soil where it happily takes rain gardens and low spots. Female plants bear small black fruit; prune in early spring only to renew density.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; one of the most cold-hardy inkberries.

directions: Sold as Nordic, this compact male selection is prized for exceptional cold tolerance and a rounded, well-branched habit. Set in moist, acidic soil and mulch to keep roots cool and evenly damp. Shear lightly in spring if a formal shape is wanted, otherwise let it mound naturally.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall near early-blooming female winterberries.

directions: This is a male pollinator, so it sets no berries but is essential for fruiting on early-flowering females like Red Sprite. Plant one within about fifty feet of the females in moist, acidic soil. It tolerates wet ground and needs only light thinning to keep an open form.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a compatible male such as Apollo.

directions: This upright hybrid of Japanese and common winterberry produces heavy, long-lasting red fruit on bare winter stems. Plant a male such as Apollo nearby for pollination and give it deep, moist, acidic soil. Cut a few branches for holiday arrangements without harming the plant.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a late-blooming male pollinator.

directions: Instead of red, this female bears pinkish-gold to orange berries that glow against snow. Pair it with a late male such as Southern Gentleman and plant in moist, acidic ground. Prune in late winter after the fruit display, cutting older canes to renew the shrub.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a late-flowering male nearby.

directions: Sold as Berry Nice, this upright female sets a heavy crop of large red berries that persist into winter. Provide a compatible late male and grow in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil. It shrugs off wet spots and needs only occasional renewal pruning.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall as a pollinator for blue hollies.

directions: A vigorous male blue holly with purplish stems and glossy dark evergreen leaves, grown mainly to pollinate female blue hollies. Plant one within reach of Blue Princess or Blue Maid in moist, well-drained, acidic soil. It sets no berries but shears well as a robust dark hedge.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a male blue holly for berries.

directions: This compact female blue holly has small, lustrous blue-green leaves and bright red berries when a male is present. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil and shelter from harsh winter wind, which can burn the foliage. Prune lightly in late winter to shape and to gather berried sprigs.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in the warm South for a berrying screen.

directions: This pyramidal hybrid carries glossy spined leaves and abundant red berries without needing a separate pollinator. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil and water regularly the first year to build a strong root system. It makes an excellent tall screen and only needs light shaping.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in the South; heat and humidity tolerant.

directions: Part of the Red Holly series, this pyramidal plant opens deep maroon-red new growth that matures to glossy green, with red berries in fall. Plant in well-drained soil and full sun to bring out the strongest leaf color. It is vigorous and heat tolerant, making a striking screen or specimen.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soils.

directions: This deciduous holly drops its leaves to reveal branches packed with long-lasting orange-red berries. Plant a male possumhaw or yaupon nearby for reliable fruit set and grow in sun for the heaviest display. Adaptable to wet or dry soils, it can be grown as a large shrub or limbed into a small tree.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall where a large evergreen is wanted.

directions: Named for its large chestnut-like serrated leaves, this vigorous holly grows into an imposing evergreen with red berries. Plant in deep, well-drained soil with room to reach tree size, and water well while establishing. It responds to pruning if a tighter form is needed but is handsome left natural.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in a spot sheltered from drying wind.

directions: This old English holly has cream-margined leaves covered in spines on both the edges and the upper surface, giving a distinctive prickly texture. It is a male clone, so it bears no berries but pollinates nearby females. Plant in well-drained soil and shield from harsh winter wind to keep the variegation clean.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall for a large glossy evergreen specimen.

directions: This female holly has large, nearly spineless glossy leaves resembling a camellia and sets bright red berries. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil with a male holly within pollinating distance for fruit. It grows into a broad pyramid and tolerates urban conditions and light shade.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a male American holly nearby.

directions: A superior female American holly with very dark, lustrous foliage and heavy sets of red berries. Plant a male such as Jersey Knight within about a hundred feet and grow in moist, acidic, well-drained soil. It matures into a large pyramidal tree, so allow generous space at planting.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall in full sun to hold golden color.

directions: This low, spreading holly has yellow-gold new foliage that is brightest in full sun and greens somewhat in shade. Plant in well-drained acidic soil and avoid soggy ground, which it dislikes. Shear lightly after the spring flush to keep a neat cushion and encourage fresh gold growth.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall with a male nearby for fruit set.

directions: This Asian deciduous holly produces masses of small, closely set red berries prized for cut branches and floral work. Plant a male within pollinating range and grow in moist, acidic soil that never fully dries out. Thin older stems in late winter to keep an open, productive framework.

planting.note: Plant spring into gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: This curious dwarf grows upright and congested with tiny overlapping leaves, making a fine miniature or bonsai subject. Plant in a rock garden or container in fast-draining soil and keep evenly moist while young. It needs essentially no pruning and matures very slowly into a dense little column.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall in well-drained soil.

directions: The classic European boxwood used for large hedges and topiary grows slowly into a dense evergreen mass. Plant with the root ball slightly high in well-drained soil and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Shear in late spring after new growth hardens, and avoid late-season pruning that leaves tender shoots.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall; good winter color retention.

directions: A hardy Sheridan hybrid forming a naturally rounded globe that keeps rich green color through winter. Plant in well-drained soil and shear once or twice in the growing season for a formal ball. Its cold tolerance and clean habit make it a dependable low hedge in northern gardens.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall; naturally upright cone shape.

directions: This upright, cone-shaped hybrid is ideal for pairs flanking an entry or for clipped cones without much shearing. Plant in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist the first year. It holds green winter color well; site out of harsh wind and afternoon winter sun to prevent bronzing.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall for a tidy low globe.

directions: This slow, compact globe needs almost no pruning to stay rounded, making it a reliable edger and knot-garden plant. Plant in well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Very hardy, it holds good green color into winter with little bronzing in sheltered sites.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; heat and sun tolerant.

directions: This littleleaf boxwood takes heat and summer sun better than most, holding deep green color even in southern gardens. Plant in well-drained soil and water through establishment, after which it is quite tough. It shears cleanly into hedges and globes and resists winter bronzing.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall; low mounding habit.

directions: This low, flat-topped, spreading boxwood is valued for cold hardiness and a blue-green cast to the new growth. Plant in well-drained soil where its mounding form can be left largely unsheared. It makes a dependable low massing plant and tolerates more cold than typical common boxwood.

planting.note: Plant spring or fall; popular hedge box for the South.

directions: A tough, heat-tolerant boxwood widely used for clipped hedges in warm regions, with light green foliage that may yellow in full winter sun. Plant in well-drained soil and shear in late spring to keep dense. Provide some afternoon shade in the hottest zones to reduce leaf scorch.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall as a narrow vertical accent.

directions: This unusually narrow, upright boxwood grows into a tall green column just a foot or so wide, perfect for tight vertical accents. Plant in well-drained soil and tie the stems loosely if snow load threatens to splay them. Little pruning is needed beyond tidying the tips.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall for a compact low hedge.

directions: A compact, densely branched boxwood with small dark leaves that keep good color and take shearing well. Plant in well-drained soil and clip lightly in late spring to maintain a formal low hedge or edging. Its tight habit and even growth make it a favorite for parterres.

planting.note: Plant spring or early fall; among the hardiest boxwoods.

directions: This very cold-hardy Korean boxwood forms a low, broad mound and may bronze slightly in winter before greening again in spring. Plant in well-drained soil and shear to shape as needed for hedges or edging. Its toughness and affordability make it one of the most widely planted boxwoods.

planting.note: Set out in spring once the ground has warmed so roots settle before summer heat.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, keeping the crown level with the surrounding soil. This mildew-resistant hybrid carries true red panicles from early summer into fall on new growth, so prune only lightly in late winter. Watch for the first flush of dark burgundy new leaves as a signal the season's bloom is underway.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, open site with room for a large vase-shaped crown.

directions: Give this fast, tall grower plenty of space, planting so the top of the root ball sits just at grade. Airy lavender-pink flower clusters open through the heat of summer, and the trunks shed to show smooth mottled bark. Remove basal suckers as they appear to keep a clean multi-trunk form.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in full sun with good air movement.

directions: Loosen the backfill and water deeply at planting to encourage a wide root run. Rich dark-red summer panicles are this old favorite's signature, though it appreciates good airflow since it can mildew in still, humid pockets. Deadheading spent trusses in midsummer often coaxes a second round of bloom.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun for the deepest flower color.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the container and mulch to hold summer moisture. Large, vivid true-red panicles cover the canopy for weeks in the heat of the season. Prune only to shape in late winter, cutting back to a bud rather than topping the main trunks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: Position the root ball level with the soil line and firm the backfill gently to remove air pockets. Bright watermelon-pink to red flower heads appear through summer and give way to good orange fall foliage. A single light thinning in late winter keeps the interior open and airy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Dig a generous planting hole and settle the shrub so the flare of the trunk stays above soil. This heritage selection carries watermelon-red to rose flower clusters across the summer months. Keep it in full sun and prune conservatively to preserve the natural upright habit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with space for a broad, arching canopy.

directions: Set the plant at grade and water in well to eliminate air gaps around the roots. Soft blush-pink panicles hang on wide, spreading branches for a graceful summer show. Its horizontal habit needs elbow room, so avoid crowding it against walls or walkways.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the fullest bloom.

directions: Give this tall white-flowered form a wide, sunny spot and plant so the root crown sits at grade. Clean white panicles blanket the tree through summer and stand out against the green foliage. Train to a few strong trunks early and remove suckers to keep a treelike shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the dark foliage can shine.

directions: Plant this compact upright selection at the depth it grew in its pot and mulch lightly. It is prized as much for its glossy burgundy-purple leaves as for the bright pink midsummer flowers. The color holds best in full sun, so avoid shady sites where the foliage greens up.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun as a low mounding shrub or border edger.

directions: Set this dwarf, mounding selection so the root ball is even with the soil surface and space plants for a low massed effect. Cherry-red flower clusters cover the compact canopy all summer, followed by red-orange fall color. A quick shearing in late winter keeps the mound dense and tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for strong flower color and mildew resistance.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball and plant at grade in a bright, open spot. This mildew-resistant Magic-series shrub bears coral-pink panicles over dark-tinged foliage through summer. Lightly thin crossing branches in late winter rather than heading the plant back hard.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant at the original soil depth and water regularly the first season to establish. Rich royal-purple flower heads emerge over glossy dark foliage and repeat well into late summer. Its compact, rounded habit suits foundation plantings and needs little pruning beyond shaping.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the plum-toned new growth.

directions: Set the shrub level with the soil and mulch to conserve moisture through the first summer. Fuchsia-pink flowers open above bronze-plum spring foliage that matures to green. Prune only lightly in late winter to keep the upright, spreading crown balanced.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at the same depth as the nursery pot and water well until established. Golden-yellow leaves with lime-green centers make this a bright foliage shrub, and small white flowers appear in summer. It takes shearing well, so trim to shape in early spring before new growth pushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to partial shade.

directions: Set the shrub level with the soil surface and mulch to keep the roots cool. Dark stems and reddish new growth set off clusters of pink-tinged white flowers from summer into fall. Prune in late winter to renew the arching stems and encourage fresh colorful growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade in average soil.

directions: Plant at grade and water regularly through the first growing season to establish roots. Creamy-margined green leaves form a tidy compact mound, with fragrant white flowers appearing in summer. Trim lightly after flowering or in early spring to maintain the neat rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to partial shade.

directions: Plant level with the soil surface and mulch to reduce summer stress. Cream, green, and pink-flushed variegated foliage gives a soft multicolor effect, joined by white summer flowers. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the low arching habit compact and full.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to partial shade in most soils.

directions: Plant the upright cane-like clumps at grade and water to settle the soil. Loose sprays of white early-summer flowers give way to showy red berries that persist through winter, and the foliage reddens in cold. Thin the tallest canes to the ground in late winter to keep the clump full at the base.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Set the plant level with the soil surface and water in well. Its constant flush of chartreuse to lime-green new leaves gives a bright, soft look and, unlike many nandinas, it seldom sets fruit. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the rounded mound dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade for strong color.

directions: Plant at grade in a sunny spot to bring out the fiery foliage. Bright red-orange new growth opens in spring, matures to green, then reddens again through fall and winter on a narrow upright form. Remove a few of the oldest canes at the base in late winter to keep it well furnished.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in partial shade with protection from harsh sun.

directions: Plant this delicate dwarf at grade and give it a sheltered site with even moisture. Very fine, threadlike foliage gives a feathery texture that turns rich red in cold weather. Its lacy leaves can scorch in intense afternoon sun, so favor morning light or dappled shade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Dig into loose, acidic soil and set the crown at grade, then mulch to hold moisture. Fiery red new leaves are the signature spring feature, following clusters of white flowers and aging to glossy green. Give it protection from drying winds and prune lightly just after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in moist, acidic soil.

directions: Plant at grade in rich acidic soil and keep the root zone evenly moist with mulch. Deep rose-red buds hold through winter and open to dark pink-red bell flowers in early spring. Site it out of harsh sun and wind, and shape only lightly after the flowers fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the shrub level with the soil in a sheltered, part-shade bed and mulch well. Soft rose-pink flowers dangle in graceful chains in early spring above bronzy new foliage. Keep the soil consistently moist but never soggy, and prune right after bloom if shaping is needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade with shelter from cold spring winds.

directions: Plant in moist acidic soil at the original depth and mulch to protect the shallow roots. New foliage emerges brilliant red, then shifts through pink and cream to green as drooping white flower panicles open in spring. Give the tender young growth shelter from late frosts and drying wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at grade in humus-rich acidic soil and keep evenly moist with a cool mulch. Leaves are edged in creamy white, and red new growth matures to show the silver margins, with white spring flowers adding to the show. Its compact size suits shaded borders and containers; shape lightly after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in moist, acidic soil.

directions: Set the root ball level with the soil surface and mulch to conserve moisture. Deep red winter buds open to pale pink-blushed white flowers, and the new foliage carries a bronze tint. Protect from hot afternoon sun and prune only lightly once the spring bloom has finished.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact selection at grade in rich acidic soil and keep the roots cool with mulch. It blooms a little later than most, with dense upright and drooping clusters of white flowers over a tidy dwarf mound. Its small size fits shaded foundations and containers, needing little pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in moist, acidic soil.

directions: Plant at grade in acidic, humus-rich soil and mulch to hold moisture around the shallow roots. Small leaves are edged in creamy white, and pink-tinged new growth gives a soft glow, with occasional white flowers in spring. This slow, compact dwarf is ideal for small shaded beds and pots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in cool, moist acidic soil and mulch well. Wine-red new leaves are the standout feature, repeating with fresh flushes through the season, and rose-pink flowers hang in early spring. Give it a sheltered part-shade site and prune lightly right after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at grade in moist acidic soil and mulch to protect the shallow root system. This hybrid pairs the hardiness of the mountain species with showy red-purple winter buds that open to white spring flowers. It resists lacebug better than many pieris, but still prefers shelter from hot sun and wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade in moist, acidic soil.

directions: Set the shrub level with the soil in a sheltered, part-shade bed and mulch to keep it cool and moist. Exceptionally long, cascading chains of pure white flowers cover the plant in early spring above bronze new growth. Water consistently through dry spells and prune only lightly after the bloom fades.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall to satisfy the long cold stratification these seeds require.

directions: Plant this stately shade tree in deep, well-drained soil with room for a broad crown to develop. Water regularly through the first few seasons until the roots reach far into surrounding ground. Small greenish-yellow flowers hang on slender stalks just as the buds break in early spring, and brilliant orange-red foliage follows in autumn.

planting.note: Fall sowing lets winter cold break the seed's dormancy naturally.

directions: Set this tough, adaptable tree in full sun where its dense canopy can spread widely. It tolerates poor soil and urban conditions but casts heavy shade that discourages grass beneath it. Clusters of showy yellow-green flowers appear before the leaves in spring, making the bare crown briefly glow.

planting.note: Autumn sowing supplies the chilling period these seeds need to sprout.

directions: This small, rounded European maple takes well to full sun and any reasonable soil, including chalk and clay. It responds beautifully to shearing, which is why it has long been used for tall hedges and screens. Modest green flowers open with the emerging leaves, and the corky-winged twigs give winter interest.

planting.note: Sow the winged seeds in fall and let the pot overwinter outdoors for cold stratification.

directions: Give this graceful ornamental a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade, sheltered from drying wind, in moist but well-drained soil. Mulch the shallow roots and water during dry spells to protect the delicate foliage from scorch. Tiny reddish-purple flowers hang in spring, and the palmate leaves flame red or orange before falling.

planting.note: Cold-stratify by sowing outdoors in fall for spring germination.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy small tree or large shrub in full sun to light shade in almost any well-drained soil. It can be grown with a single trunk or as a multi-stemmed specimen and needs little pruning once established. Fragrant creamy flowers open with the leaves in spring, followed by showy red samaras and fiery scarlet fall color.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seeds receive their winter chilling before sprouting.

directions: This adaptable small maple thrives in full sun and tolerates dry, alkaline, and compacted soils that trouble larger species. Grow it as a low branching tree or clump for patios and tight sites. Upright clusters of white spring flowers give way to bright red-winged seeds that color the crown through summer.

planting.note: Autumn sowing meets the cold requirement for germination the following spring.

directions: A large, wind-firm shade tree that stands up to coastal salt spray and exposed sites where other maples struggle. Plant in full sun in deep soil and allow ample space for the dense, domed crown. Hanging chains of green-yellow flowers dangle in late spring, and it self-sows freely from its winged seeds.

planting.note: Sow in fall and overwinter the seeds outdoors to break dormancy.

directions: Set this rounded Japanese species in dappled shade with steady moisture and shelter from harsh wind. Its broad, nearly circular leaves have many shallow lobes and turn vivid crimson and gold in autumn. Showy nodding clusters of purple-red flowers appear with the unfurling foliage in spring.

planting.note: Set out balled or containerized trees in spring or fall while soil is workable.

directions: This vigorous hybrid of red and silver maple combines fast growth with strong branch structure, so plant it in full sun with room to fill out. Water deeply during establishment and mulch the root zone to keep it cool. Reddish flowers open before the leaves in early spring, and dependable scarlet fall color is its main attraction.

planting.note: These stubborn seeds often need warm then cold periods, so sow in fall and be patient.

directions: A slow-growing trifoliate maple prized for its soft, three-part leaves and burgundy autumn tones. Give it moist, well-drained soil in sun or light shade and shelter from drying wind. Small yellow-green flowers hang in threes in spring, and the neat, spreading habit suits smaller gardens.

planting.note: Sow fresh in fall; germination is slow and often waits a second spring.

directions: This choice small tree is grown as much for its shaggy, peeling amber bark as for its brilliant orange-red fall leaves. Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in sun to part shade. Flowers appear in threes in spring, giving the tree its common name, atop soft trifoliate foliage.

planting.note: Fall sowing supplies the cold stratification these seeds need.

directions: A snakebark maple grown for its green-and-white striped bark and long, drooping strings of spring flowers. Site it in moist, well-drained soil in light shade with protection from the hottest afternoon sun. The unlobed leaves turn yellow and orange in fall, and the striated trunk carries the display through winter.

planting.note: Sow in fall to give seeds the winter chill they require.

directions: This Japanese snakebark shows off green bark striped silvery-white on young stems flushed with red. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil in part shade where the striped trunk can be admired. Pendulous racemes of small green flowers open in spring, and the lobed leaves turn fiery red in autumn.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall so winter cold breaks dormancy.

directions: Also called Japanese painted maple, this round-headed tree resembles a refined Norway maple with smaller, cleaner leaves. Grow it in full sun to part shade in any well-drained soil. Yellow-green flowers appear with the new leaves in spring, and foliage turns clear yellow before dropping in fall.

planting.note: Autumn sowing gives seeds the cold period needed to germinate.

directions: A drought-tolerant Mediterranean maple with small, glossy three-lobed leaves and a dense, rounded crown. Plant in full sun in well-drained, even dry or alkaline soil, where it shrugs off heat and poor conditions. Small yellow-green flowers open in spring, followed by pinkish samaras that ripen to brown.

planting.note: Sow in fall to satisfy the seed's stratification requirement.

directions: This handsome round-crowned tree bears broad, glossy five- to seven-lobed leaves that emerge red and turn butter-yellow in fall. Plant in full sun in deep, moist, well-drained soil and give it space to develop a wide canopy. Yellow flowers appear in spring, and the tree suckers freely from the roots.

planting.note: Cold-stratify by sowing outdoors in fall for spring sprouting.

directions: One of the most unusual maples, its unlobed, deeply veined leaves look exactly like those of a hornbeam. Plant this shrubby tree in moist, well-drained soil in part shade for the best foliage. Hanging clusters of green flowers open in spring, and the leaves turn warm brown-yellow in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted specimens in spring or fall in a sheltered, part-shade site.

directions: This slow, compact maple carries soft, rounded chartreuse leaves that glow golden-yellow all season in the right light. Site it in bright dappled shade, since full sun scorches the delicate foliage and deep shade dulls the color. Small red flowers stand upright above the leaves in spring, an unusual charm of this species.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seeds get the prolonged cold they need.

directions: A cold-hardy trifoliate maple that is one of the earliest trees to color, turning brilliant red in late summer. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil. Delicate green flowers hang in threes in spring above the slender, three-part leaves on red stalks.

planting.note: Fall sowing provides the cold stratification these seeds require.

directions: A tough, upright shade tree resembling hedge maple but larger, with corky bark and broad lobed leaves. Plant in full sun in ordinary well-drained soil, where it tolerates urban stress and cold well. Yellow-green flowers appear with the leaves in spring, and foliage turns soft yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant balled or container trees in spring or fall while soil works easily.

directions: This popular hybrid selection is prized for fast growth and dependable, uniform orange-red autumn color. Plant in full sun with room for its broad oval crown and water deeply until established. Reddish flowers appear before the leaves in early spring, and its strong central leader resists the storm damage that plagues silver maple.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A red maple selection famous for holding its glossy foliage late and finishing with intense crimson fall color. Plant in full sun in moist, slightly acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Clusters of tiny red flowers stud the bare branches in earliest spring before any leaves appear.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall while the ground is workable and moist.

directions: This widely planted red maple colors earlier than most, glowing orange-red to brilliant red in autumn. Give it full sun and moist, acidic, well-drained soil for the strongest display and vigor. Small red flowers open on bare twigs in very early spring, one of the first signs of the season.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: This Norway maple holds deep maroon-purple foliage from spring until leaf drop, a bold contrast in the landscape. Plant in full sun in almost any soil, as it tolerates urban conditions well but casts dense shade. Yellow flowers tinged with red open before the leaves and glow against the emerging purple crown.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in full sun and average soil.

directions: A fast, upright Norway maple grown as a uniform street and shade tree with a dense oval crown. Plant in full sun in any reasonable soil, allowing space since the canopy shades out lawn beneath it. Yellow-green flowers appear before the leaves in spring, and dark green summer foliage turns yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: A heat- and drought-tolerant sugar maple selection with a dense oval crown and thick, leathery leaves that resist scorch. Plant in full sun in deep, fertile soil with good drainage. Yellow-green flowers hang as the buds break in spring, and the foliage turns rich orange-red in fall.

planting.note: Set out balled or container trees in spring or fall.

directions: This vigorous sugar maple has thick, glossy leaves that hold up to summer heat and wind better than the species. Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil and water through establishment. Small greenish flowers appear with the emerging leaves in spring, and reliable orange-scarlet color comes in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: This upright red maple leafs out later in spring, letting it dodge late frosts that damage other purple cultivars. Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade, with afternoon shelter in hot climates. Reddish flowers appear in spring, and the dark purple-red leaves turn brilliant scarlet in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled shade with rich, moist soil.

directions: This slow, compact fullmoon maple carries rounded leaves in shades of burnt orange, gold, and rosy peach that shift through the season. Plant in bright dappled shade in moist, well-drained soil, giving more sun for warmer tones and more shade for chartreuse. Small red flowers stand upright above the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Grown for its deeply cut, fern-like leaves and outstanding orange-red-crimson autumn display, this is one of the finest fullmoon maples. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil in dappled shade with shelter from wind. Showy nodding clusters of red-purple flowers hang beneath the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A narrow, columnar red maple ideal for streets, tight yards, and formal rows where a broad crown will not fit. Plant in full sun in moist, slightly acidic soil and water through establishment. Tiny red flowers stud the bare branches in early spring, and the upright form colors orange-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and moist soil.

directions: A rounded, seedless red maple selected for late, long-lasting red fall color and good resistance to leafhoppers. Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil. Because it is a male clone it sets no messy seed, and small red flowers still open on bare twigs in early spring.

planting.note: Sow in fall to give seeds the cold period they need to sprout.

directions: An Italian and southern European maple notable for opening clusters of showy yellow flowers before the leaves emerge. Plant in full sun in well-drained, even chalky soil where it tolerates dry conditions once established. The broadly lobed leaves turn yellow in fall on this rounded, medium-sized tree.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural stratification through winter.

directions: A small, nearly evergreen Mediterranean maple with tiny leathery leaves that persist through mild winters. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where it tolerates heat, drought, and coastal exposure. Small yellow-green flowers open in spring, and the dense, twiggy habit suits screens and dry gardens.

planting.note: Sow stratified acorns in autumn or set out balled saplings before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant acorns about one inch deep in loose, well-drained loam, or set nursery stock with the root flare level to the soil surface. This fast-growing shade tree tolerates a range of soils but resents heavy clay and standing water. Insignificant catkins appear in spring as leaves expand, with acorns maturing over two seasons.

planting.note: Sow fresh acorns in fall where the tree is to grow, since deep taproots resist transplanting.

directions: Push acorns just under the surface in dry, rocky, or sandy ground, where this ridge-top oak thrives on poor sites. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant once settled but hard to move, so start it in place. Yellow-green catkins bloom in spring, followed by large sweet acorns favored by wildlife.

planting.note: Sow acorns after the first fall rains so seedlings root before summer drought returns.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in fast-draining soil and keep summer irrigation away from the trunk to avoid root rot. This broad evergreen California native forms a rounded canopy and needs almost no water once established. Spring catkins are followed by slender pointed acorns.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn on well-drained slopes where the tree naturally grows.

directions: Set acorns about one inch deep in gritty, well-drained soil, mimicking the canyon walls where this evergreen oak grows wild. Golden fuzz coats the acorn cups and young leaf undersides, giving the tree its name. It is slow-growing, drought-hardy, and tolerant of both heat and mountain cold.

planting.note: Sow acorns after fall rains so roots establish before the dry season.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in well-drained inland soils and withhold summer water once established. This evergreen holds glossy, sometimes spiny-edged leaves through the year and forms a dense rounded crown. Spring catkins give way to long, slender acorns that mature over two years.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring in the arid Southwest, as they ripen and sprout in early summer.

directions: Plant acorns shallowly in gravelly, fast-draining desert-mountain soil and give only occasional deep water. This semi-evergreen oak of the Southwest bears sweet, edible acorns known locally as bellotas. It tolerates intense heat and drought once its deep roots are established.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in dry mountain soils; plants often sucker into thickets.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in well-drained rocky soil where this cold-hardy western oak forms shrubby to small-tree colonies. It spreads by root sprouts, so allow room or expect a thicket. Deep green summer leaves turn russet and gold in autumn.

planting.note: Sow ripe acorns in early summer in gravelly Southwest soils.

directions: Set acorns just below the surface in coarse, well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted. This large evergreen oak of the desert mountains carries thick, blue-green, leathery leaves. It withstands heat, drought, and light frost with ease.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring to early summer in dry, rocky Southwest terrain.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in gritty limestone or granite soils where this small evergreen oak grows on arid slopes. Its gray-green, sandpapery leaves resist heat and drought. Give it full sun and minimal supplemental water.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring in fast-draining canyon soils of the Southwest.

directions: Plant acorns shallowly in well-drained, gravelly soil in full sun. The narrow evergreen leaves are glossy green above and strikingly white-woolly beneath, giving a two-toned shimmer in the breeze. It handles heat, drought, and cold desert-mountain nights.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn in well-drained soil in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This graceful small-to-medium oak from the Near East holds narrow, sharply toothed, chestnut-like leaves. It is prized for its fine texture and tolerance of hot, dry summers.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn in dry, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in lean, well-drained soil and give full sun. This tough Balkan oak is semi-evergreen in mild climates and thrives on hot, dry limestone hillsides. Its small, holly-toothed leaves and drought tolerance make it a good choice for warm, arid gardens.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in acidic, well-drained soil; trees may sucker.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in acidic, sandy loam in full sun. This southwestern-European oak bears deeply lobed, softly downy leaves and often forms suckering groves. It is heat- and drought-tolerant and marcescent, holding tawny dead leaves into winter.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn on dry, calcareous soils in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in dry, limey, well-drained soil in full sun. This southern-European oak is named for the soft down covering its young shoots and leaf undersides. Slow-growing and long-lived, it excels on hot, drought-prone slopes.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn in deep, moist, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in fertile, deep soil in full sun. This vigorous semi-evergreen oak from North Africa and Iberia holds large, lobed leaves late into winter. It grows quickly and tolerates a wide range of soils.

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery stock in fall or early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Set grafted trees with the graft union above soil level in deep, fertile, well-drained ground. This vigorous semi-evergreen hybrid between cork and Turkey oak keeps much of its foliage through mild winters. It develops a broad, stately crown and thick, corky bark with age.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in well-drained soil; tolerates coastal and urban sites.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This dense, small evergreen oak from East Asia carries small, glossy, toothed leaves and takes readily to shearing as a hedge. It tolerates salt, wind, and urban conditions.

planting.note: Sow acorns in autumn in dry, rocky, alkaline soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in dry, stony, well-drained ground in full sun. This shrubby Mediterranean evergreen oak bears small, spiny, holly-like leaves and forms dense thickets. Extremely drought-tolerant, it thrives on the harshest, driest limestone sites.

planting.note: Sow acorns after fall rains in well-drained soil in a mild, dry-summer climate.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in well-drained soil and avoid summer irrigation near the trunk. This uncommon southern-California oak holds blue-green, mostly untoothed evergreen leaves. Slow-growing and drought-adapted, it forms a broad, spreading canopy.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in well-drained soil in a mild, coastal-influenced climate.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. This rare evergreen oak from the California Channel Islands carries handsome, corrugated, holly-like leaves with felted undersides. It appreciates coastal fog and moderate water more than most western oaks.

planting.note: Sow ripe acorns in spring in gravelly, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant acorns shallowly in coarse, well-drained soil in full sun. This evergreen oak of the Southwest and Mexico is named for the strongly netted veins on its thick, cupped leaves. It withstands heat and drought and stays fairly compact.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in deep, dry sand in full sun. This small semi-evergreen oak of southeastern scrub and coastal dunes holds smooth-edged, leathery leaves. It is highly drought- and salt-tolerant, suiting sandy coastal gardens.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring in gravelly soil in the arid Southwest.

directions: Plant acorns shallowly in fast-draining desert-grassland soil in full sun. This small evergreen oak bears smooth, oblong, blue-gray leaves and forms a rounded crown. It is very drought-tolerant and well suited to hot, arid landscapes.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in well-drained limestone soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in rocky, alkaline, well-drained soil in full sun. This tough Texas Hill Country oak emerges with peachy new growth that matures to blue-green. Compact and heat-tolerant, it is a favorite small shade tree for dry limestone gardens.

planting.note: Sow stratified acorns in spring in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun. This small red oak from the mountains of west Texas and Mexico turns bright red in fall, a rarity for the arid Southwest. It is heat- and drought-tolerant once established.

planting.note: Sow stratified acorns in fall in acidic, sandy, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant acorns about an inch deep in dry, sandy, acidic soil in full sun. This cold-hardy midwestern oak resembles pin oak but tolerates drier upland sites. Deeply lobed leaves turn a good russet-red in autumn.

planting.note: Sow fresh acorns in fall in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This East Asian white oak bears large, coarsely scalloped leaves with whitish undersides. It grows into a broad, handsome shade tree in a wide range of temperate gardens.
